behaviors.description: The generated reply draft contains specific references (e.g., customer name, problem description, dates) from the incoming email thread, rather than generic boilerplate text.
behaviors.justification: This behavior depends on real LLM non-determinism. A mocked LLM that always returns 'thank you for your email' will pass a test even if the real LLM generates generic responses for complex threads. The failure 'LLM does not produce context-aware responses' is only detectable by calling the real LLM and verifying it actually extracts and references specific details. Different model versions, temperatures, or prompt variations may produce different levels of specificity.
behaviors.test_description: Call the real LLM with a multi-message email thread (e.g., customer reports specific bug on line 42, mentions March 30 deadline, references prior incident from February). Verify the draft contains specific references to at least 3 details (exact line number, deadline date, prior incident). Run 3–5 times with different thread scenarios to confirm context-awareness is not accidental.

behaviors.description: The factual consistency check flags claims in the draft that directly contradict entries in the knowledge base (e.g., 'Company X was founded in 2020' contradicts KB entry 'Company X founded 2010').
behaviors.justification: This behavior tests the consistency-checking logic against a real knowledge base using deterministic algorithms (rule matching, embeddings, or semantic similarity). The failure 'checker does not flag an obvious contradiction' is architectural and caught by integration testing with real KB data and actual checker logic. However, if the consistency checker uses LLM-based entailment, the minimum would be Agentic.
behaviors.test_description: Populate a knowledge base with 5 facts (founding dates, names, relationships). Create drafts that contradict 3 facts and align with 2 facts. Assert the checker flags exactly the 3 contradictions and accepts the 2 aligned claims. Test both obvious mismatches ('2020' vs '2010') and semantic contradictions ('company is 10 years old' contradicts 'founded 2014' in year 2024).

behaviors.description: When the knowledge base has no relevant entries for a topic, the draft includes an explicit disclaimer (e.g., 'I don't have sufficient information about this') rather than fabricating or inferring facts.
behaviors.justification: This behavior exposes LLM hallucination—the failure is 'LLM invents facts instead of admitting uncertainty.' A mocked LLM that always includes disclaimers will pass the test even if the real LLM frequently hallucinates. Only a real LLM call reveals whether the model actually respects the 'admit uncertainty' instruction or generates plausible-sounding false claims. Hallucination rates vary across model versions and temperature settings.
behaviors.test_description: Populate a KB with entries only about 'Customer Support Policies'. Query the LLM to draft a reply about 'Advanced Machine Learning Techniques' (topic with zero KB coverage). Verify the draft includes uncertainty language ('I don't have information', 'not in my knowledge base') rather than ML claims. Run 5 times to check for consistent refusal to fabricate. Flag and measure any hallucinated technical details.

behaviors.description: No draft is sent automatically; the approval workflow enforces an explicit user approval step before any email can be dispatched.
behaviors.justification: The failure 'draft is sent without user approval' is a logic and workflow error caught by testing the approval gate with real gate logic and mocking the send operation. Integration testing verifies the approval check is enforced at all code paths before send. System testing with real email infrastructure is safer for operational assurance, but logical requirement (gate blocks send) is testable at Integration.
behaviors.test_description: Create a draft in the system. Attempt to trigger send without approval and verify the send function is not called (e.g., mock email.send is never invoked). Approve the draft and verify send is called with correct recipient and content. Test both API/UI endpoints and internal workflow to confirm approval is checked at all send decision points.

behaviors.description: The complete pipeline—reading an incoming email thread, generating a context-aware draft via LLM, checking factual consistency, and presenting for user approval—executes successfully for a realistic email scenario.
behaviors.justification: End-to-end execution requires at least one real LLM call (draft generation), which is Agentic. Although thread parsing, consistency checking, and approval can be deterministic, the draft generation is the critical path and its correctness depends on real LLM behavior. A mock LLM hides issues like poor prompt engineering or incorrect context injection. Testing with real email data and real LLM reveals integration failures and behavioral inconsistencies across the pipeline.
behaviors.test_description: Load a realistic multi-message email thread (3–4 messages with technical details, tone context, customer name, issue description). Run the full pipeline: parse thread, generate draft with real LLM, check consistency against a sample KB, present approval workflow. Verify the draft references specific thread details, flags any contradictions, includes disclaimers where KB is empty, and approval cannot be bypassed. Test with 2–3 diverse scenarios (technical issue, billing inquiry, follow-up) to validate generalization.
